title: [FIRRTL] Add pass to handle grand central data and memory taps
body:
This PR adds the `GrandCentralDataTaps` pass that consumes the various SiFive data and memory tap annotations. These are generally added to `firrtl.extmodule`s and assign arbitrary signals or ports in the design with ports of the module. The compiler then generates a body for the module, assigning the ports to the corresponding signals or ports pulled up from the design via SystemVerilog hierarchical names. This is a first shot at this.

I'm opening this in parallel to @seldridge's #1170, but we could just as well eventually merge this into the more general `GrandCentralPass` over there. Having a separate pass first allows for development to be a bit more decoupled.

### Quirks

There are a bunch of quirks with this implementation:

These annotations have a lot of quirks. For one, even though the intent is to have a single point in the hierarchy (an *instance*) that gathers random signals in the design for inspection (i.e. concrete instances), the annotations are made on *modules*. This works in the Chisel/CHIRRTL case, since every instance in the design has a dedicated "copy" of the module, so modules and instances are equivalent in CHIRRTL. If we allow modules to be instantiated multiple times (which is the only sensible path forward), these annotations become quirky and the pass has to make an educated guess which signals the user is actually referring to. A piece of future work might be to try and change the way these annotations are generated upstream (annotate instances and concrete signals rather than modules).

Another quirk of this PR is that it (ab)uses `sv.verbatim.expr` and `firrtl.stdIntCast` to assemble a hierarchical name in the FIRRTL dialect. This is horribly brittle and definitely not what we want to have in the long run. FIRRTL should probably gain a hierarchical reference operation that can use @darthscsi's work on adding symbols to things to actually specify a clean chain of symbols as a hierarchical name that would be properly lowered through HW to SV and to Verilgo output.

Finally, the mem taps would like to access individual words of a memory. The Chisel side would just assume that memories generate a storage array in Verilog that carry their name. However, in CIRCT we are making a push for different options of how memories are lowered (e.g. the out-of-line memories we currently emit, external generators, etc.). We currently lack the information to dig into these representations and pull out the storage array (although that may usually be possible, with some pain). This suggests that we might need a dedicated operation that encodes this intent into the IR.

### Alternative Implementation

This PR currently sticks to the Scala side very closely (besides trying to rectify the module-instance equivalence constraint). In doing so, it tries to generate a `firrtl.module` for a `firrtl.extmodule` annotated as data tap, which comes with various issues in terms of visibility of symbols. A different approach would be to replace the *instance* of the data tap extmodule with an operation that encodes the hierarchical access of the tapped information. So rather than having a weird triangular setup where the black box extmodule has to guess what's available in its parent and digging from there, the module that wants to have data tapped could do so directly. This would simplify generating the hierarchical names.

### Todo
- [x] Land #1218
